title: At the Gym
summary: A new gym member asks a trainer for advice on starting a fitness routine, and they discuss which machines to use, how often to exercise, and the importance of warming up.

body.speaker: Member
body.text: Hi, I just joined the gym yesterday and I'm not sure where to start.

body.speaker: Trainer
body.text: Welcome! Don't worry — I can help you put together a simple routine.

body.speaker: Member
body.text: That would be great. My main goal is to get a bit fitter and lose some weight.

body.speaker: Trainer
body.text: Good goal. How many days a week can you come to the gym?

body.speaker: Member
body.text: Probably three days a week. Maybe Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.

body.speaker: Trainer
body.text: Three days is perfect for a beginner. Always start with a ten-minute warm-up on the treadmill.

body.speaker: Member
body.text: Should I run or just walk on the treadmill?

body.speaker: Trainer
body.text: Just walk at a brisk pace for now. Running is fine later when you feel more comfortable.

body.speaker: Member
body.text: Okay. And after the warm-up, what should I do?

body.speaker: Trainer
body.text: I recommend the rowing machine and the stationary bike. Both are great for your heart and easy on your joints.

body.speaker: Member
body.text: How long should I spend on each machine?

body.speaker: Trainer
body.text: About fifteen to twenty minutes each. And always finish with a cool-down stretch.

body.speaker: Member
body.text: I didn't know stretching was so important. Why does it matter?

body.speaker: Trainer
body.text: Stretching helps your muscles recover and prevents soreness the next day. Trust me — you'll thank me later!

annotations.meaningVi: lập kế hoạch / xây dựng một thói quen tập luyện
annotations.exampleEn: The coach helped me put together a training routine.

annotations.meaningVi: trở nên khoẻ hơn một chút
annotations.exampleEn: I want to get a bit fitter before the summer.
She decided to eat less and exercise more to lose some weight.
Always do a five-minute warm-up before any exercise.
She runs five kilometres on the treadmill every morning.

annotations.meaningVi: tốc độ nhanh, bước đi nhanh nhẹn
annotations.exampleEn: Walking at a brisk pace is good for your cardiovascular health.

annotations.meaningVi: máy chèo thuyền (dụng cụ tập thể dục)
annotations.exampleEn: The rowing machine gives you a full-body workout.

annotations.meaningVi: nhẹ nhàng với các khớp (không gây áp lực lớn lên khớp)
annotations.exampleEn: Swimming is easy on your joints and great for older adults.

annotations.meaningVi: bài tập giãn cơ sau khi tập (để hạ nhiệt cơ thể)
annotations.exampleEn: A cool-down stretch after running helps reduce muscle pain.
Rest days give your muscles time to recover after hard training.

annotations.meaningVi: ngăn ngừa đau nhức cơ bắp
annotations.exampleEn: Drinking enough water after exercise prevents soreness.

grammarNotes.title: Modal 'should' for advice and recommendations
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Should + verb nguyên thể' dùng để đưa ra lời khuyên hoặc gợi ý. Ví dụ: 'Should I run?' / 'What should I do?' / 'You should start with a warm-up.' Để phủ định, ta dùng 'shouldn't': 'You shouldn't run too fast at first.' Đây là cấu trúc rất phổ biến khi nói về sức khoẻ và tập luyện.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'should + base verb' to give or ask for advice. Examples: 'Should I run on the treadmill?', 'You should always warm up first.' For negative advice use 'shouldn't': 'You shouldn't skip your cool-down.' This modal is essential in health and fitness conversations.

grammarNotes.title: Frequency expressions: days a week
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'X days a week' diễn tả tần suất trong một tuần. Ví dụ: 'three days a week' = ba ngày mỗi tuần, 'twice a week' = hai lần một tuần. Các cụm tương tự: 'once a month', 'every other day'. Những cụm này thường đứng ở cuối câu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'X days a week' to express how often something happens in a week. Examples: 'I go to the gym three days a week.', 'She swims twice a week.' Similar patterns: 'once a month', 'every other day'. These frequency phrases usually appear at the end of a sentence.

translationVi:
Hội viên: Chào, tôi vừa tham gia phòng gym hôm qua và không biết bắt đầu từ đâu.
Huấn luyện viên: Chào mừng bạn! Đừng lo — tôi có thể giúp bạn lập một kế hoạch tập luyện đơn giản.
Hội viên: Vậy thì tuyệt quá. Mục tiêu chính của tôi là trở nên khoẻ hơn một chút và giảm cân.
Huấn luyện viên: Mục tiêu hay đấy. Bạn có thể đến phòng gym bao nhiêu ngày một tuần?
Hội viên: Có thể là ba ngày một tuần. Có lẽ là thứ Hai, thứ Tư, và thứ Sáu.
Huấn luyện viên: Ba ngày là hoàn hảo cho người mới bắt đầu. Luôn luôn bắt đầu với mười phút khởi động trên máy chạy bộ.
Hội viên: Tôi có nên chạy hay chỉ đi bộ trên máy chạy bộ thôi?
Huấn luyện viên: Chỉ cần đi bộ với tốc độ nhanh nhẹn thôi. Chạy thì được sau này khi bạn cảm thấy thoải mái hơn.
Hội viên: Được rồi. Và sau khi khởi động, tôi nên làm gì?
Huấn luyện viên: Tôi khuyên bạn nên dùng máy chèo thuyền và xe đạp cố định. Cả hai đều rất tốt cho tim và nhẹ nhàng với các khớp.
Hội viên: Tôi nên dành bao nhiêu thời gian cho mỗi máy?
Huấn luyện viên: Khoảng mười lăm đến hai mươi phút mỗi máy. Và luôn kết thúc bằng bài tập giãn cơ.
Hội viên: Tôi không biết giãn cơ lại quan trọng như vậy. Tại sao nó quan trọng?
Huấn luyện viên: Giãn cơ giúp cơ bắp hồi phục và ngăn ngừa đau nhức vào ngày hôm sau. Tin tôi đi — sau này bạn sẽ cảm ơn tôi!

questions.prompt: When did the member join the gym?
questions.explanation: The member says 'I just joined the gym yesterday.'
questions.hint: The member says exactly when they joined.

questions.prompt: What is the member's main fitness goal?
options: To get fitter and lose some weight
questions.explanation: The member says 'My main goal is to get a bit fitter and lose some weight.'

questions.prompt: How many days a week does the member plan to go to the gym?
questions.explanation: The member says 'Probably three days a week.'
questions.hint: The member suggests specific days to help remember.

questions.prompt: What should the member always do first at the gym?
options: Start with a ten-minute warm-up on the treadmill
questions.explanation: The trainer says 'Always start with a ten-minute warm-up on the treadmill.'
questions.hint: The trainer gives a clear first instruction.

questions.prompt: What does the trainer say the member should do on the treadmill for now?
options: Just walk at a brisk pace
questions.explanation: The trainer says 'Just walk at a brisk pace for now.'
questions.hint: The trainer says running can come later.

questions.prompt: Which two machines does the trainer recommend after the warm-up?
questions.explanation: The trainer recommends 'the rowing machine and the stationary bike.'
questions.hint: One machine simulates water sport; the other simulates cycling.

questions.prompt: Why does the trainer recommend those two machines?
options: They are the most popular in the gym
They are good for the heart and easy on joints
They help build arm strength quickly
questions.explanation: The trainer says they are 'great for your heart and easy on your joints.'
questions.hint: Two reasons are given — one about the heart, one about joints.

questions.prompt: How long should the member spend on each machine?
questions.explanation: The trainer says 'About fifteen to twenty minutes each.'
questions.hint: A range of time is suggested.

questions.prompt: What should the member always do at the end of the session?
questions.explanation: The trainer says 'always finish with a cool-down stretch.'
questions.hint: It involves stretching your body to finish the workout.

questions.prompt: According to the trainer, why is stretching important?
options: It helps muscles recover and prevents soreness
questions.explanation: The trainer says 'Stretching helps your muscles recover and prevents soreness the next day.'
questions.hint: Two benefits are explained — recovery and prevention of pain.

questions.prompt: What does the trainer say at the very end of the conversation?
options: Trust me — you'll thank me later!
Come back tomorrow for another session
Good luck — you won't need it!
questions.explanation: The trainer ends with 'Trust me — you'll thank me later!'
questions.hint: It is a confident, friendly expression of reassurance.

cloze.template:
Member: Hi, I just joined the gym yesterday and I'm not sure where to {{b1}}.
Trainer: Welcome! Don't worry — I can help you put together a simple {{b2}}.
Member: That would be great. My main goal is to get a bit fitter and lose some {{b3}}.
Trainer: Good goal. How many days a week can you come to the gym?
Member: Probably three days a week. Maybe Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.
Trainer: Three days is perfect for a {{b4}}. Always start with a ten-minute warm-up on the treadmill.
Member: Should I run or just walk on the treadmill?
Trainer: Just walk at a brisk {{b5}} for now. Running is fine later when you feel more comfortable.
Member: Okay. And after the warm-up, what should I do?
Trainer: I recommend the rowing machine and the stationary {{b6}}. Both are great for your heart and easy on your joints.
Member: How long should I spend on each machine?
Trainer: About fifteen to twenty minutes each. And always finish with a cool-down {{b7}}.
Member: I didn't know stretching was so important. Why does it matter?
Trainer: Stretching helps your muscles {{b8}} and prevents {{b9}} the next day. Trust me — you'll {{b10}} me later!

blanks.explanation: 'start' — the member doesn't know where to start their gym journey.
'routine' — a routine is a regular set of exercises done in a fixed order.
'weight' — the member wants to lose some weight.
'beginner' — someone who is new to an activity; the trainer says three days is perfect for a beginner.
'pace' — pace means speed of movement; a brisk pace is a fast walk.
'bike' — the stationary bike is one of the two recommended machines.
'stretch' — a cool-down stretch at the end of exercise is important.
'recover' — stretching helps muscles recover after exercise.
'soreness' — muscle soreness is the aching feeling after exercise.
'thank' — the trainer says the member will thank them later for the good advice.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The trainer tells the member to start slowly with walking before running. In learning new skills — whether physical or mental — is it always better to start slowly and build up gradually? Can starting too fast ever be beneficial? Give reasons for your view.
